The **Hockey Champions Trophy** (**HCT**) was an international [field hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey "Field hockey") tournament held by the [International Hockey Federation](/wiki/International_Hockey_Federation "International Hockey Federation") (FIH). It featured the top 6 ranked nations in that year.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hockey_Champions_Trophy&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Founded in 1978 by Pakistan's Air Marshal [Nur Khan](/wiki/Nur_Khan "Nur Khan") and the [Pakistan Hockey Federation](/wiki/Pakistan_Hockey_Federation "Pakistan Hockey Federation"), it featured the world's top-ranked [field hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey "Field hockey") teams competing in a [round robin](/wiki/Round-robin_tournament "Round-robin tournament") format. A biennial women's tournament was added in [1987](/wiki/1987_Women%27s_Hockey_Champions_Trophy "1987 Women's Hockey Champions Trophy"). The Champions Trophy was changed from an annual to a biennial event from 2014 onwards, due to the introduction of the [Hockey World League](/wiki/FIH_Hockey_World_League "FIH Hockey World League") (HWL). The 2018 edition was the last edition of the Champions Trophy and the tournament was replaced by the [Men's FIH Pro League](/wiki/Men%27s_FIH_Pro_League "Men's FIH Pro League") and the [Women's FIH Pro League](/wiki/Women%27s_FIH_Pro_League "Women's FIH Pro League") in 2019.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

In the men's tournament, [Australia](/wiki/Australia_men%27s_national_field_hockey_team "Australia men's national field hockey team") won the tournament fifteen times, [Germany](/wiki/Germany_men%27s_national_field_hockey_team "Germany men's national field hockey team") ten and the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands_men%27s_national_field_hockey_team "Netherlands men's national field hockey team") eight times. [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan_men%27s_national_field_hockey_team "Pakistan men's national field hockey team") is the only Asian champion, with three titles to its name including the first two in 1978 and 1980. In the women's tournament, [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina_women%27s_national_field_hockey_team "Argentina women's national field hockey team") and the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands_women%27s_national_field_hockey_team "Netherlands women's national field hockey team") won the trophy seven times each. [Australia](/wiki/Australia_women%27s_national_field_hockey_team "Australia women's national field hockey team") have won the trophy six times, while [Germany](/wiki/Germany_women%27s_national_field_hockey_team "Germany women's national field hockey team"), [China](/wiki/China_women%27s_national_field_hockey_team "China women's national field hockey team") and [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea_women%27s_national_field_hockey_team "South Korea women's national field hockey team") have won it one time each.

Since the 2011 edition, eight teams of each six have qualified for the championship. The first edition had five teams, the second had seven, 1987 had eight, and all other editions through 2010 had six. In the year following the [Olympics](/wiki/Field_hockey_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Field hockey at the Summer Olympics") or a [World Cup](/wiki/Hockey_World_Cup "Hockey World Cup"), the participating teams include the host, the defending champion, the world champion and the next highest ranked teams from either the most recent World Cup or Olympic Games.
